info.name: Rely Health Take-Home — AI Outbound Calling System
info.description:
Full API surface for the AI outbound calling take-home: an operator console that places real outbound appointment-reminder calls via Retell, tracked through a webhook-driven 5-state machine (see backend `state_machine.py` / project `CLAUDE.md`).

Three folders:
1. **Patients** — the read-only list the operator picks a call target from.
2. **Calls** — placing a call and checking its status, including the 404 paths.
3. **Events (Webhooks)** — the `POST /events` webhook receiver, split into **Legal** and **Illegal** subfolders. This folder exists specifically so the state machine's behavior — both correctly applying real transitions and correctly rejecting impossible/out-of-order ones — can be demonstrated live, on a local Postgres row, **without needing a real Retell phone call or a cloudflared tunnel**. Every Illegal request's description explains exactly how to seed a `call_logs` row into the right starting status first (via a `docker exec` SQL snippet against the local Postgres container), since the illegal scenarios specifically require states that the live app either passes through too quickly to catch (e.g. `connecting`) or that would collide with the webhook dedup constraint if reached via the normal request chain.

**No real patient data, phone numbers, or API keys anywhere in this collection.** All examples use synthetic `+1555…` seeded patient numbers or deliberately fake UUIDs.

Set the collection variable `base_url` to point at your running backend (defaults to `http://localhost:8000`, i.e. the Docker Compose backend service exposed on the host).

variable.description: Backend base URL. Default matches `docker compose up`'s host port mapping (8000:8000).

A synthetic seeded patient id (Maria Gonzalez, +15555550101) from this project's `python -m app.seed_patients` fixture data, captured from a live dev DB. Patient ids are randomly generated (uuid4()) at seed time, so this exact value is specific to the environment it was captured from — if this 404s against your own dev DB, run 'GET /patients' first, copy the id of any patient whose phone_number starts with +1555, and overwrite this variable.

A real completed call_id captured from this project's dev DB (resolved to closed, with outcome_reason later upgraded to 'voicemail (detected late)' by a call_analyzed event — see the project README's outcome_reason design note for the full story). Like synthetic_patient_id, this is environment-specific — if it 404s for you, substitute the call_id returned by the 'Place a call' request in the Calls folder, or any call_id produced by the Events folder's Legal scenarios.

item.name: Patients
item.description: Read-only patient list the operator console renders and picks a call target from. No auth, no pagination (see CLAUDE.md Non-goals) — this is a demo-scoped internal tool, not a public API.

request.description: Returns every Patient row (id, name, date_of_birth, phone_number, appointment_date/time, timezone). This is the source list the frontend's patient table renders, and the easiest way to grab a fresh `synthetic_patient_id` value if the collection variable default has gone stale for your environment (pick any row whose phone_number starts with +1555).

item.name: Calls
item.description: Placing a call and polling its status, including both success and 404 paths for each endpoint.

request.description:
Places a call for a synthetic `+1555…` seeded patient. **This is safe to run repeatedly — it never places a real phone call and never costs money.** `+1555…` isn't a real routable number, so Retell's own request validation rejects it (HTTP 400) before any call is attempted; the app synchronously marks the `CallLog` row `connection_failed` with `outcome_reason: "invalid_request"` and returns 201 with that status in the body (this endpoint always returns 201, even on a provider failure — the *body's* `status` field is what tells you what happened; see `CLAUDE.md`'s 'DB write before provider call' sequencing decision for why).

This demonstrates the `connecting → connection_failed` branch of the state machine live, and is also how you can grab a fresh, real `call_id` to feed into the 'GET /calls/{id}' request below.

If `synthetic_patient_id` 404s in your environment (ids are randomly generated at seed time), run 'GET /patients' in the folder above first and copy any +1555 patient's id into that collection variable.

response.name: 201 Created — connection_failed (real captured response)
response.body:
{
  "call_id": "6041129a-3ae6-455c-b551-743ce70bbf26",
  "patient_id": "bc7c4930-4924-4603-ae87-92e8cfc1b7fc",
  "provider_call_id": null,
  "status": "connection_failed",
  "started_at": "2026-07-12T22:02:21.452570Z",
  "ended_at": null,
  "error_reason": "Client error '400 Bad Request' for url 'https://api.retellai.com/v2/create-phone-call'",
  "outcome_reason": "invalid_request"
}

request.description: Uses an obviously fake, never-real UUID to demonstrate the 404 path — no `CallLog` row is created for a patient that doesn't exist.

Defaults to a real completed call from this project's dev DB (`closed`, with `outcome_reason` upgraded from `agent_hangup` to `voicemail (detected late)` by a later `call_analyzed` event — see the project README's `outcome_reason` design note for the full story). If `example_call_id` doesn't exist in your environment, substitute the `call_id` returned by 'Place a call' above, or any `call_id` from the Events folder's Legal scenarios.
Uses an obviously fake, never-real UUID to demonstrate the 404 path.

item.name: Events (Webhooks)
item.description:
The `POST /events` webhook receiver — the endpoint Retell delivers `call_started` / `call_ended` / `call_analyzed` events to. This folder is designed to demonstrate the state machine **live, end to end, without a real Retell call or a cloudflared tunnel**: every request below targets a `call_logs` row you seed directly in the local Postgres container via a one-line `docker exec ... psql` command (given in each request's description), then fires a real webhook payload at the real endpoint and lets the app's actual logic (dedup, legal-transition check, status/outcome_reason update) run for real. Nothing here is mocked — only the *source* of the row (direct SQL insert instead of a real Retell-placed call) is substituted, exactly the same pattern this project's own pytest fixtures (`make_call_log`) use.

Generic cleanup, safe to run any time: `docker exec ai_calling_agent_postgres psql -U postgres -d ai_calling_agent -c "DELETE FROM call_logs WHERE provider_call_id LIKE 'demo-%'; DELETE FROM webhook_events WHERE provider_call_id LIKE 'demo-%';"`

Split into ✅ Legal (transitions the state machine allows) and ❌ Illegal (transitions it correctly rejects) — see each subfolder's own description.

item.name: ✅ Legal scenarios
item.description:
Five real payload shapes this project has actually observed from Retell (trimmed to only the fields the app reads — real Retell payloads carry many more fields, e.g. agent_id, transcript, recording_url, none of which the app currently uses). Each demonstrates a transition the state machine allows; expect `{"status": "applied"}` for anything that changes `CallLog.status`, or `{"status": "recorded"}` for `call_analyzed`, which is informational-only by design and never changes status (see CLAUDE.md's event → state mapping table).

'call_started' and 'call_ended (user_hangup)' are designed to run in sequence against the **same** seeded row (`demo-user-hangup`), walking it through its full real lifecycle: dialing → ongoing → closed. The other three scenarios each use their own dedicated, independently-seeded row.

request.description:
**Prerequisite (run once before this request):**
```
docker exec ai_calling_agent_postgres psql -U postgres -d ai_calling_agent -c "INSERT INTO call_logs (call_id, patient_id, provider_call_id, status, started_at) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), (SELECT id FROM patients LIMIT 1), 'demo-user-hangup', 'dialing', now());"
```
Seeds a fresh row in `dialing` (the state a real call reaches right after Retell's sync API accepts it, before any webhook arrives). Firing this `call_started` webhook against it is the real payload shape Retell sends when a call actually connects — `call.call_status: "ongoing"` in the body confirms it. Expected: `{"status": "applied"}`, row moves to `ongoing`.

Run **'call_ended (user_hangup)'** below next, against this same row, to see it walk all the way to `closed`.

Run **after** 'call_started' above, against the same `demo-user-hangup` row (must already be `ongoing`, which only `call_started` can produce). `disconnection_reason: "user_hangup"` is a normal hangup, which maps to `closed` (not `no_response`) per CLAUDE.md's event → state mapping table. Expected: `{"status": "applied"}`, row moves to `closed`, `outcome_reason` set to `"user_hangup"`, `ended_at` populated from `event_timestamp`.

Verify with 'GET /calls/{id}' (Calls folder) using the `call_id` from the SQL insert, or query directly: `docker exec ai_calling_agent_postgres psql -U postgres -d ai_calling_agent -c "SELECT status, outcome_reason FROM call_logs WHERE provider_call_id = 'demo-user-hangup';"`

**Prerequisite:**
```
docker exec ai_calling_agent_postgres psql -U postgres -d ai_calling_agent -c "INSERT INTO call_logs (call_id, patient_id, provider_call_id, status, started_at) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), (SELECT id FROM patients LIMIT 1), 'demo-no-answer', 'dialing', now());"
```
Own dedicated row (independent of the user_hangup pair above). `dial_no_answer` is one of the two `disconnection_reason` values mapped to `no_response` (nobody picked up — direct `dialing → no_response` is a legal single hop, unlike `closed`, which requires passing through `ongoing`). Expected: `{"status": "applied"}`, row moves to `no_response`, `outcome_reason` set to `"dial_no_answer"`.

**Prerequisite:**
```
docker exec ai_calling_agent_postgres psql -U postgres -d ai_calling_agent -c "INSERT INTO call_logs (call_id, patient_id, provider_call_id, status, started_at) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), (SELECT id FROM patients LIMIT 1), 'demo-voicemail', 'dialing', now());"
```
Own dedicated row. `voicemail_reached` is Retell's *real-time* voicemail-detection signal on `call_ended` — the other reason mapped to `no_response` alongside `dial_no_answer`. This is distinct from `call_analysis.in_voicemail`, the *retrospective* signal that only arrives later on `call_analyzed` (see the next scenario, and the project README's voicemail-mapping note, for how these two can genuinely diverge). Expected: `{"status": "applied"}`, row moves to `no_response`, `outcome_reason` set to `"voicemail_reached"`.

**Prerequisite:**
```
docker exec ai_calling_agent_postgres psql -U postgres -d ai_calling_agent -c "INSERT INTO call_logs (call_id, patient_id, provider_call_id, status, started_at, ended_at, outcome_reason) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), (SELECT id FROM patients LIMIT 1), 'demo-analyzed', 'closed', now(), now(), 'user_hangup');"
```
Seeds a row that's *already* `closed` (as if a real `call_ended` already resolved it) with `outcome_reason: "user_hangup"`, simulating a call that looked like a normal hangup in real time. `call_analyzed` never changes `status` — it's informational-only by design (see CLAUDE.md). But if `call_analysis.in_voicemail` is `true` and the existing `outcome_reason` doesn't already say `voicemail_reached`, the app upgrades `outcome_reason` to `"voicemail (detected late)"` — exactly the real divergence this project discovered live (a call that looked like `closed`/hangup in real time, later confirmed via transcript analysis to actually have been voicemail; see 'GET /calls/{id} — completed call' above and the README's outcome_reason note). Expected: `{"status": "recorded"}` (not `"applied"` — status is untouched), but querying the row afterward shows `outcome_reason` changed.

item.name: ❌ Illegal scenarios
item.description:
Two payloads the state machine correctly rejects — recorded to `webhook_events` for forensic auditability (per CLAUDE.md: 'illegal/duplicate transitions are still recorded... but not applied to CallLog'), but never applied to the `CallLog` row. Both are verified end to end against the real running app before being included here.

Expected response for both: `{"status": "recorded"}`, with the row's `status` unchanged afterward. Each request's description gives the exact SQL to seed a fresh row in the right starting state — see each one for why the *specific* seeding approach used is necessary (it's not always as simple as reusing a row from the Legal folder).

request.description:
**Why it's illegal:** per `state_machine.py`'s `ALLOWED_TRANSITIONS`, `connecting`'s only legal next states are `dialing` and `connection_failed` — there is no direct path from `connecting` to `closed` (or `no_response`), because a call must pass through `dialing` (confirmed by Retell's sync API) before any webhook can possibly apply. This event skips straight past `ongoing` entirely.

**Getting a fresh row into `connecting` first:** this is the one scenario where you can't get there via the live app — `POST /patients/{id}/call` writes `connecting` and then resolves the provider call *synchronously within the same request*, so by the time you can observe it, it's already moved past `connecting`. Seed it directly instead:
```
docker exec ai_calling_agent_postgres psql -U postgres -d ai_calling_agent -c "INSERT INTO call_logs (call_id, patient_id, provider_call_id, status, started_at) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), (SELECT id FROM patients LIMIT 1), 'demo-illegal-connecting', 'connecting', now());"
```
**Expected:** `{"status": "recorded"}`. Verify the row is still `connecting` (untouched): `docker exec ai_calling_agent_postgres psql -U postgres -d ai_calling_agent -c "SELECT status FROM call_logs WHERE provider_call_id = 'demo-illegal-connecting';"`

**Why it's illegal:** `closed` has no legal next states at all (it's terminal) — this simulates a late/out-of-order `call_started` webhook arriving after the call has already fully resolved, which must not move status backward or sideways.

**Getting a fresh row into `closed` first — read this carefully:** it's tempting to reuse the `demo-user-hangup` row from the Legal folder (already `closed` after running 'call_started' + 'call_ended (user_hangup)' there) and simply fire `call_started` at it again. **Don't** — that row already received one real `call_started` event, so a second one hits the `(event_type, provider_call_id)` webhook dedup constraint *first* and returns `{"status": "duplicate_ignored"}` instead of demonstrating the illegal-transition rejection path (confirmed by actually running it this way). To see the real `{"status": "recorded"}` illegal-transition response, seed a **fresh** row directly at `closed`, with a `provider_call_id` that has never seen a `call_started` event:
```
docker exec ai_calling_agent_postgres psql -U postgres -d ai_calling_agent -c "INSERT INTO call_logs (call_id, patient_id, provider_call_id, status, started_at, ended_at, outcome_reason) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), (SELECT id FROM patients LIMIT 1), 'demo-illegal-closed', 'closed', now(), now(), 'user_hangup');"
```
**Expected:** `{"status": "recorded"}`. Verify the row is still `closed` (untouched): `docker exec ai_calling_agent_postgres psql -U postgres -d ai_calling_agent -c "SELECT status FROM call_logs WHERE provider_call_id = 'demo-illegal-closed';"`
